note.soap:
S:  
34-year-old adult presents with a tick bite sustained in Georgia. The tick was attached for approximately 37 hours. The patient reports an expanding bullseye-like rash consistent with erythema migrans. No systemic symptoms such as fever, chills, severe headache, body aches, or stiff neck are present.

O:  
No physical exam or vital signs available due to phone-only encounter.

A:  
- Working diagnosis (most likely): Early Lyme disease with erythema migrans rash following tick bite.  
- Differential: Other tick-borne illnesses less likely given absence of systemic symptoms and presence of classic rash.  
- Red flags considered: No systemic symptoms reported (fever, chills, severe headache, body aches, stiff neck).

P:  
- Disposition: Outpatient management appropriate given stable presentation and absence of systemic symptoms.  
- Plan:  
  Recommended regimen; reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated:  
  - Doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 10 days (first-line adult treatment).  
  - Alternatives if doxycycline contraindicated: amoxicillin 500 mg PO three times daily for 14 days OR cefuroxime axetil 500 mg PO twice daily for 14 days.  
  - If unable to take above antibiotics, azithromycin 500 mg PO daily for 7 days (less effective; use only if needed).  
- Safety net: Advise patient to seek urgent care if they develop new or worsening symptoms such as fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, facial weakness, palpitations, shortness of breath, or neurological deficits.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.
